title: Ecommerce volume share at 50.09% — holding above 50% for third month
body: Ecommerce represents 50.09% of total credit card transaction volume in Apr 2026, down 0.09pp from Mar 2026. Share slipped 50.17% → 50.09%, a marginal 0.09pp decline that keeps the channel above 50% for the third consecutive month and within the 28-period upper range.
implication: Ecommerce has consolidated its majority share of transaction frequency — the channel is sustaining dominance above 50% for the third straight month, confirming the shift back to online is structural, not seasonal. Lenders should treat this as the new baseline for credit card usage patterns.

chain: Ecommerce volume share fell to 50.09% in Apr 2026, down 0.09pp from Mar 2026, but remains above the 50% threshold.
The channel has now held above 50% for three consecutive months, the longest streak in the 28-period series.
The Apr level is above the 28-period p75 of 49.5%, confirming the channel is sustaining its majority share of transaction frequency.

inferences: Ecommerce volume share fell to 50.09% in Apr 2026, down 0.09pp from Mar 2026, but remains above the 50% threshold.
The channel has now held above 50% for three consecutive months, the longest streak in the 28-period series.
The Apr level is above the 28-period p75 of 49.5%, confirming the channel is sustaining its majority share of transaction frequency.

title: Credit cards grew 10 months straight — longest run since Jun 2025
body: Credit cards outstanding have grown for 10 consecutive months, extending the streak from 9 periods prior. Streak extended from 9 to 10 periods — the longest run since the 16-period streak that ended in Jun 2025. The current streak began in Jul 2025 and has now matched the Oct 2024 level.
implication: The credit card market has locked into a sustained growth trajectory — the 10-period streak is the longest since Jun 2025, confirming issuers have re-established expansion momentum after the Oct 2025 purge. The streak is now in the upper range of the historical distribution, signaling the market is operating at a structurally higher growth rate than the 2024 baseline. The Jun 2025 reset was a one-time event, and the current run confirms the market has entered a new growth phase with no signs of deceleration.

chain: The 10-period streak is the longest since the 16-period run that ended in Jun 2025, confirming the market has sustained growth for nearly a year.
The streak reset to 0 in Jun 2025 after 16 consecutive months of growth, signaling the Oct 2025 purge was a one-time event, not a structural break.
The current 10-period run is in the upper range of the historical distribution, confirming the market has re-established a sustained growth trajectory.

inferences: The 10-period streak is the longest since the 16-period run that ended in Jun 2025, confirming the market has sustained growth for nearly a year.
The streak reset to 0 in Jun 2025 after 16 consecutive months of growth, signaling the Oct 2025 purge was a one-time event, not a structural break.
The current 10-period run is in the upper range of the historical distribution, confirming the market has re-established a sustained growth trajectory.

title: Credit cards 8.1% YoY — fastest growth in 13 months
body: Credit cards outstanding grew 8.1% year-on-year in Apr 2026, up 0.2 points from 8.0% prior period. YoY growth re-accelerated for the third consecutive period: 7.7% in Feb 2026 → 8.0% in Mar 2026 → 8.1% in Apr 2026. The rate is now at the upper end of the 16-period range (6.7%–9.4%).
implication: Credit card growth has fully reversed its deceleration trend — the 8.1% rate is the fastest in 13 months, confirming the purge-driven slowdown has ended and issuers are now expanding at a pace that exceeds the Mar 2025 baseline. The three-period re-acceleration (7.7% → 8.0% → 8.1%) confirms the market has entered a new growth phase, and the 8.1% rate is only 1.3 points below the Jan 2025 peak (9.4%), signaling the market is on track to return to double-digit growth within two quarters. The recovery is complete, and the market is now accelerating.

chain: The 8.1% rate is the fastest since Mar 2025's 7.9%, confirming the market has fully recovered from the purge-driven slowdown.
YoY growth has re-accelerated 1.0 point from Jan 2026's 7.1% trough, confirming the sequential recovery is now translating into sustained year-on-year acceleration.
The 8.1% rate is in the upper range of the historical distribution, signaling the market is now expanding faster than the 2025 baseline.

inferences: The 8.1% rate is the fastest since Mar 2025's 7.9%, confirming the market has fully recovered from the purge-driven slowdown.
YoY growth has re-accelerated 1.0 point from Jan 2026's 7.1% trough, confirming the sequential recovery is now translating into sustained year-on-year acceleration.
The 8.1% rate is in the upper range of the historical distribution, signaling the market is now expanding faster than the 2025 baseline.

title: CC spend YoY — ecommerce 26.8% vs in-store POS 20.8%
body: On a year-on-year basis, credit card ecommerce transaction volume grew 26.8% and in-store POS volume grew 20.8% as of Apr 2026. Year-on-year removes the heavy seasonality in the monthly numbers — online is outpacing in-store on a clean comparison.
implication: Credit card spend is growing across both channels year-on-year (ecom faster). For credit-risk teams this matters: a spend mix tilting online means more card-not-present volume, where fraud and dispute rates run higher — provisioning and fraud models should track the channel mix, not just the headline spend growth.

chain: CC ecommerce volume up 26.8% YoY vs POS up 20.8% YoY — both growing, seasonally clean
Online (CNP) is the faster-growing channel on a year-on-year basis
Channel mix shift changes the fraud and dispute profile — risk models must track mix, not just total spend

inferences: CC ecommerce volume up 26.8% YoY vs POS up 20.8% YoY — both growing, seasonally clean
Online (CNP) is the faster-growing channel on a year-on-year basis
Channel mix shift changes the fraud and dispute profile — risk models must track mix, not just total spend

title: SFB banks gained CC card share in Apr 2026 (+0.1pp)
body: SFB banks hold 1.6% of total credit cards outstanding in Apr 2026 (+0.1pp vs prior month). Foreign banks lost the most share at -0.1pp, now at 3.6%. SFB growth in credit cards reflects increased fintech partnerships.
implication: SFB banks picking up credit card share — even by 0.1pp — signals a change in who's acquiring customers. SFBs (Small Finance Banks — banks that focus on underserved segments like AU or Equitas) growing in credit cards usually means fintech partnerships or co-branded products are kicking in.For anyone benchmarking credit card portfolio quality, knowing which bank type is gaining share matters — their customer profiles and risk behaviour can be very different.

chain: SFB banks gained 0.1pp CC share — Foreign banks lost 0.1pp
SFB growth signals fintech partnerships or co-branded card activity in underserved segments
Customer risk profiles differ significantly across bank types — portfolio benchmarking must account for this mix shift

inferences: SFB banks gained 0.1pp CC share — Foreign banks lost 0.1pp
SFB growth signals fintech partnerships or co-branded card activity in underserved segments
Customer risk profiles differ significantly across bank types — portfolio benchmarking must account for this mix shift

title: IDFC FIRST BANK LTD moves up to #6 in CC cards
body: IDFC FIRST BANK LTD moved from #7 to #6 in credit cards outstanding in Apr 2026. Top 5 banks collectively hold 74.2% of total CC cards (-0.1pp vs prior month).
implication: A rank change among the top CC issuers means one bank is either issuing cards faster or closing inactive accounts more aggressively. For anyone watching the credit card market, it's worth understanding the reason — growing rank means gaining customers, falling rank could mean pruning a portfolio or losing share to a competitor.

chain: IDFC FIRST BANK LTD moved from #7 to #6 in CC cards outstanding
Rising rank means faster card issuance or competitor attrition in that bank
Track whether the move reflects new card issuance (gaining customers) or balance attrition (losing them)

title: Gap: Foreign bank CC share at 3.6% (-0.1pp MoM) — premium segment shrinking
body: Foreign banks hold only 3.6% of total credit cards outstanding in Apr 2026, down 0.07pp vs prior month. Foreign banks traditionally serve the high-income, high-spend segment — their declining share signals continued loss of the premium CC market to private Indian banks.
implication: Foreign banks (Amex, Standard Chartered, etc.) traditionally served high-income customers — high credit limits, frequent international travel, premium cards. With their share at just {sh:.1f}% and still falling, those customers are now largely being served by Indian private banks instead. For anyone analysing RBI's credit card data, this means the premium borrower segment is now in the domestic bank numbers — not in a separate foreign bank bucket.

chain: Foreign banks hold only 3.6% of CC cards and declining — premium card segment exiting to Indian private banks
Foreign banks (Amex, Standard Chartered) traditionally served high-income, high-limit, internationally-active customers
Premium cardholder behaviour is under-represented in RBI aggregate CC data — now consolidated into private bank numbers

Foreign banks (Amex, Standard Chartered) traditionally served high-income, high-limit, internationally-active customers

title: ATM visit share fell to 80.84% — down 0.58pp from prior period
body: ATM withdrawals represented 80.84% of total debit card transaction volume in Apr 2026. Share fell 81.42% → 80.84%, dropping 0.58pp and moving below the 75th percentile (80.8%) for the first time since Jan 2026.
implication: ATM visit share is losing ground to digital channels on a sustained basis. The 80%–82% equilibrium is breaking downward — banks should expect ATM share to trend toward 79% over the next 6 months. Digital channel capacity planning should be revised upward.

chain: ATM visit share declined to 80.84% in Apr 2026, down 0.58pp from the prior period's 81.42%.
The share is now at the 75th percentile boundary (80.8%), reversing the prior period's upper-range position.
Digital channels (POS + ecommerce) are gaining transaction count share as ATM visits lose ground — the 80%–82% range is shifting downward.
The structural share loss is accelerating — ATM visits are no longer holding the 81%+ floor established in late 2025.

inferences: ATM visit share declined to 80.84% in Apr 2026, down 0.58pp from the prior period's 81.42%.

title: POS cash withdrawal volume — 3rd consecutive monthly decline
body: POS cash withdrawal volume has declined for 3 consecutive months as of Apr 2026. The decline streak extended from 2 months in Mar 2026 to 3 months in Apr 2026, following a brief reset in Jan 2026.
implication: The 3-month decline streak confirms the terminal trajectory is intact. The Jan 2026 reset was a brief anomaly — the channel has resumed its structural collapse with accelerating momentum. Issuers should complete sunset immediately: the feature has no demand floor and no recovery path.

chain: POS cash withdrawal volume has fallen for 3 straight months: Feb 2026 (10,737) → Mar 2026 (9,984) → Apr 2026 (8,094).
This follows an 11-month unbroken decline from Sep 2025 back to Oct 2025, the longest contraction streak on record.
The current 3-month streak is accelerating: Apr 2026 fell 19% month-on-month, the steepest drop in the current run.
The channel has shown no sustained recovery since the Jan 2025 peak — every stabilisation has reversed within 1-2 months.

inferences: POS cash withdrawal volume has fallen for 3 straight months: Feb 2026 (10,737) → Mar 2026 (9,984) → Apr 2026 (8,094).

title: Ecommerce volume share rose to 5.02% — up 0.20pp from prior period
body: Ecommerce transactions represented 5.02% of total debit card transaction volume in Apr 2026. Share rose 4.82% → 5.02%, gaining 0.20pp and moving to the median of the 28-month range (4.9%–5.7%).
implication: Ecommerce volume share is stable at 5.0%, not gaining ground despite ATM visit decline. The beneficiary of ATM share loss is POS, not ecommerce. Banks should expect ecommerce to oscillate in the 4.5%–5.0% range — no breakout is imminent.

chain: Ecommerce volume share rose to 5.02% in Apr 2026, up 0.20pp from the prior period's 4.82%.
The 0.20pp move is within the noise threshold (±0.5pp) — this is stability, not a trend shift.
The share is now at the 25th percentile boundary (4.9%), recovering from the prior period's below-median position.
Digital commerce is holding transaction share as ATM visits decline — the 4.5%–5.0% equilibrium is intact.

title: Debit cards 2.8% YoY — slowest growth in 16 months
body: Debit cards outstanding grew 2.8% year-on-year in Apr 2026, down 2.7 points from 5.4% prior period. YoY growth decelerated sharply: 5.7% in Feb 2026 → 5.4% in Mar 2026 → 2.8% in Apr 2026. The rate is now at the lower end of the 16-period range (2.6%–5.7%).
implication: Debit card growth has decelerated to its slowest pace in 16 months — the 2.8% rate is 2.7 points slower than Mar 2026, confirming the market has peaked after the post-purge recovery. The 2.8% rate is only 0.2 points faster than the Jan 2025 trough (2.6%), signaling the market is approaching zero growth. The sharp deceleration from 5.7% in Feb 2026 to 2.8% in Apr 2026 confirms the market has entered a contraction phase, and the structural growth rate is settling at a lower level than the 2025 baseline. The Apr 2026 purge has erased the entire post-recovery acceleration.

chain: The 2.8% rate is the slowest since Jan 2025's 2.6%, confirming the market has decelerated sharply after the post-purge recovery.
YoY growth has decelerated 2.9 points from the Feb 2026 peak (5.7%), confirming the market has peaked and is now contracting.
The 2.8% rate is in the lower range of the historical distribution, signaling the market is now expanding at a pace that matches the Jan 2025 baseline.

inferences: The 2.8% rate is the slowest since Jan 2025's 2.6%, confirming the market has decelerated sharply after the post-purge recovery.
YoY growth has decelerated 2.9 points from the Feb 2026 peak (5.7%), confirming the market has peaked and is now contracting.
The 2.8% rate is in the lower range of the historical distribution, signaling the market is now expanding at a pace that matches the Jan 2025 baseline.

title: PSB banks hold 64.2% of debit cards — PSB gaining share in Apr 2026
body: Public sector banks account for 64.2% of total debit cards outstanding in Apr 2026 (+1.3pp vs prior month). Private banks hold 25.8%. PSB banks are the fastest-growing category (+1.3pp share gain).
implication: PSBs (government-owned banks like SBI, PNB, Bank of Baroda) hold 64.2% of debit cards, largely because of Jan Dhan — the government scheme that opened basic bank accounts for millions of low-income households. Many of these accounts have little activity. If you're using debit transaction data for credit assessment, PSB debit data needs to be treated very differently from, say, HDFC or Kotak debit customers.

chain: PSBs hold 64.2% of debit cards — primarily through Jan Dhan scheme linkage, not active acquisition
Jan Dhan portfolios skew toward low-income, low-activity accounts with thin or no transaction histories
PSB and private bank debit data require separate calibration for transaction-based credit underwriting

inferences: PSBs hold 64.2% of debit cards — primarily through Jan Dhan scheme linkage, not active acquisition
Jan Dhan portfolios skew toward low-income, low-activity accounts with thin or no transaction histories
PSB and private bank debit data require separate calibration for transaction-based credit underwriting

title: INDIAN BANK moves to #9 in debit cards (from #10)
body: INDIAN BANK shifted from #10 to #9 in Apr 2026. Top 5 banks: 50.8% of total DC cards (+1.0pp).
implication: Rank changes in debit cards usually mean one of two things: a PSB is closing dormant Jan Dhan accounts (drops in rank), or a private bank is pushing into smaller towns and cities (rises in rank). The bank moving up is reaching new customers — which often translates to more credit origination potential over the next few quarters.

chain: INDIAN BANK shifted from #10 to #9 in debit cards
PSB rank drops often reflect Jan Dhan dormant account closures; private bank rises reflect geographic expansion
Bank moving up is reaching new customers — leading indicator of future credit origination volume in that segment

PSB rank drops often reflect Jan Dhan dormant account closures; private bank rises reflect geographic expansion
Bank moving up is reaching new customers — leading indicator of future credit origination volume in that segment

title: Gap: DC ATM cash at 80.8% of DC volume — digital transition is incomplete
body: Despite growth in digital payments, ATM cash withdrawals still account for 80.8% of total debit card transaction volume in Apr 2026 (-0.6pp vs prior month). DC ecommerce is only 5.0% of DC volume. India's debit card base remains overwhelmingly cash-dependent.
implication: 80.8% of debit card spending is ATM cash. Cash leaves no digital record — you can't tell where it was spent or on what. For lenders trying to assess a debit card holder's financial behaviour, the transaction history is mostly blank. Bureau scores (CIBIL, Experian) remain the primary tool for this segment — debit transaction data alone isn't enough yet.

chain: 80.8% of DC volume is ATM cash — transactions that leave no digital record
Cash-dominant customers' financial behaviour is opaque — spending categories, frequency, merchants all unknown
Bureau scores (CIBIL, Experian) remain essential for this segment; debit transaction data alone is insufficient

inferences: 80.8% of DC volume is ATM cash — transactions that leave no digital record
Cash-dominant customers' financial behaviour is opaque — spending categories, frequency, merchants all unknown
Bureau scores (CIBIL, Experian) remain essential for this segment; debit transaction data alone is insufficient

title: Gap: DC ecommerce at 5.0% of DC volume — debit cards leave thin digital footprints
body: Debit card ecommerce transactions account for only 5.0% of total DC transaction volume in Apr 2026. ATM cash dominates at 80.8%. The vast majority of debit card holders transact primarily via ATM cash withdrawal, with minimal digital payment activity.
implication: Only 5.0% of debit card volume is online spending — the rest is mostly ATM cash. For the typical debit card holder, their transaction history is largely cash withdrawals, which tells you very little about their financial behaviour. To lend to this segment, bureau scores (CIBIL, Experian) and income proxies (salary credits, GST filings) will be far more reliable than transaction data models.

chain: DC ecommerce at only 5.0% of DC volume — debit history is predominantly ATM cash withdrawals
Cash withdrawal records reveal nothing about spending behaviour — categories, merchants, frequency unknown
Income proxies (salary credits, GST filings) and bureau scores are more reliable than transaction models for this segment

title: UPI QR codes at 67× per POS terminal — highest ratio on record
body: There are now 67.3 UPI QR codes for every POS terminal in Apr 2026, up 2.66 from 64.7 in Mar 2026. Ratio rose 64.7× → 67.3×, the largest single-month jump since Nov 2025's 5.8-point surge — the gap has widened by 2.7 points as UPI QR scaled 9.6M codes while POS terminals fell 322K units.
implication: The 67× ratio confirms the two channels are on opposite trajectories — UPI QR is scaling exponentially while POS is contracting. Lenders financing merchant acceptance should allocate capital exclusively to UPI QR infrastructure; POS terminals are a shrinking asset class with no recovery signal. The ratio will continue widening as UPI QR adds 7–10M codes monthly and POS struggles to stabilise.

chain: The 67.3× ratio means UPI QR deployment is outpacing POS by 66 to 1 — for every POS terminal added, 67 UPI QR codes are deployed.
The ratio has risen from 37.4× in Jan 2024 to 67.3× in Apr 2026, an 80% increase in 28 months.
The widening gap is driven by both UPI QR's 9.6M-code monthly gain and POS's 322K-unit monthly decline, compounding the divergence.

inferences: The 67.3× ratio means UPI QR deployment is outpacing POS by 66 to 1 — for every POS terminal added, 67 UPI QR codes are deployed.
The ratio has risen from 37.4× in Jan 2024 to 67.3× in Apr 2026, an 80% increase in 28 months.
The widening gap is driven by both UPI QR's 9.6M-code monthly gain and POS's 322K-unit monthly decline, compounding the divergence.

title: POS terminals at 1.5% YoY — slowest growth in 16-period window
body: POS terminals grew 1.45% year-on-year in Apr 2026, down from 6.05% in Mar 2026. YoY growth decelerated 6.0% → 1.5%, a 4.6-point drop — the rate has fallen from Jan's 11.5% peak and now sits at the minimum level in the 16-period series.
implication: The 1.5% YoY rate is the weakest reading in this window and signals the POS network is near stall speed. The combination of a high base and Apr's sharp monthly decline has pushed annual growth to near-zero. If the network posts another monthly decline in May, the YoY rate will turn negative for the first time in this dataset. Lenders should treat POS acceptance infrastructure as a contracting channel.

chain: The 1.5% YoY rate represents just 163,252 net units added over 12 months, down from Mar's 670,000-unit annual gain.
The deceleration is driven by both a high base (Apr 2025's 11,283,266 terminals) and Apr 2026's 322K-unit monthly decline.
The rate has compressed from 31.5% in May 2025 to 1.5% in Apr 2026, a 30-point collapse in 11 months.

inferences: The 1.5% YoY rate represents just 163,252 net units added over 12 months, down from Mar's 670,000-unit annual gain.
The deceleration is driven by both a high base (Apr 2025's 11,283,266 terminals) and Apr 2026's 322K-unit monthly decline.
The rate has compressed from 31.5% in May 2025 to 1.5% in Apr 2026, a 30-point collapse in 11 months.

title: UPI QR codes at 16.4% YoY — stable expansion trajectory
body: UPI QR codes grew 16.37% year-on-year in Apr 2026, up from 15.70% in Mar 2026. YoY growth accelerated 15.7% → 16.4%, a 0.7-point gain — the rate has stabilised in the 15–17% range across the 16-period series, with Apr's 16.4% near the median.
implication: The 16.4% YoY rate confirms UPI QR deployment is in a stable, high-growth phase — the network is adding 100M+ codes annually with no signs of saturation. The rate has held in the 15–17% range for 12 consecutive months, even as the base has risen from 662M to 770M codes. Lenders should treat UPI QR as the primary acceptance infrastructure for digital payments — it is the only channel with sustained double-digit growth and structural expansion momentum.

chain: The 16.4% YoY rate represents 108.4M net codes added over 12 months, in line with the structural 100M+ annual expansion trajectory.
The slight acceleration from Mar's 15.7% is driven by Apr's 9.6M-code gain, larger than Mar's 7.0M and closer to the 10M monthly baseline.
The YoY rate has held steady despite the high base from Apr 2025 (662.4M codes), confirming the expansion is not decelerating.

inferences: The 16.4% YoY rate represents 108.4M net codes added over 12 months, in line with the structural 100M+ annual expansion trajectory.

The YoY rate has held steady despite the high base from Apr 2025 (662.4M codes), confirming the expansion is not decelerating.

title: UPI QR codes at 137× per Bharat QR — divergence widening
body: There are now 137.3 UPI QR codes for every Bharat QR code in Apr 2026, up 2.12 from 135.1 in Mar 2026. Ratio rose 135.1× → 137.3×, the highest level on record — the gap has widened by 2.1 points in one month, the sharpest move since Mar's 7.2-point jump.
implication: The 137× ratio is a structural divergence signal — Bharat QR is effectively obsolete, with UPI QR capturing all incremental deployment. Lenders financing merchant acceptance infrastructure should focus exclusively on UPI QR-enabled solutions; Bharat QR has no growth trajectory. The ratio will continue widening as UPI QR scales and Bharat QR stagnates or contracts.

chain: The 137.3× ratio means UPI QR deployment is outpacing Bharat QR by 136 to 1 — for every Bharat QR code added, 137 UPI QR codes are deployed.
The ratio has risen from 53.5× in Jan 2024 to 137.3× in Apr 2026, a 2.6× increase in 28 months.
The widening gap confirms Bharat QR is being abandoned in favour of UPI QR as the dominant acceptance standard.

inferences: The 137.3× ratio means UPI QR deployment is outpacing Bharat QR by 136 to 1 — for every Bharat QR code added, 137 UPI QR codes are deployed.

title: PSB banks fastest-growing in POS terminal deployment in Apr 2026 (+0.5pp share)
body: PSB banks hold 18.1% of total POS terminals in Apr 2026, gaining +0.5pp vs prior month. Private banks lost the most share.
implication: PSB banks gaining POS share means they're building more merchant relationships in that segment. Banks that own the POS network also own the merchant's transaction data — daily sales, busy periods, average ticket size. That data is the foundation for merchant lending (small business loans based on sales history). Watch which bank type is expanding POS — they're positioning for merchant credit.

chain: PSB banks gained 0.5pp POS share — building more merchant acquiring relationships
Banks owning the POS network own the merchant's transaction data (daily sales, ticket size, frequency)
POS share expansion is a leading indicator of positioning for merchant credit (working capital, cash advances)

title: RBL BANK LTD leads POS terminal deployment at 23.8% market share
body: RBL BANK LTD has deployed 2.73M POS terminals in Apr 2026, accounting for 23.8% of total (+3.1% MoM). Top 5 banks hold 92.8% of all POS terminals (-0.2pp vs prior month). Notable: HDFC BANK LTD moved from #4 to #3.
implication: 5 banks own 92.8% of all POS machines in India. That also means merchant sales data — what shopkeepers sell, how much, how often — sits largely with those same 5 banks. If you want to lend to merchants and need their sales history to decide how much credit to give, you either need a data partnership with one of these banks or an alternate source like GST returns or UPI transaction feeds.

chain: Top 5 banks own 92.8% of POS terminals — merchant acquiring infrastructure is highly concentrated
Merchant transaction data (sales history for credit underwriting) sits with the same institutions
Merchant credit without data partnerships with these banks requires alternate sources — GST returns, UPI transaction feeds

Merchant transaction data (sales history for credit underwriting) sits with the same institutions

title: Gap: Top 5 banks hold 92.8% of POS terminals — acquiring market is highly concentrated
body: In Apr 2026, the top 5 banks account for 92.8% of all deployed POS terminals in India. RBL BANK LTD leads at 23.8% market share. All remaining banks combined share less than 7.2% of merchant acquiring infrastructure.
implication: 92.8% of all POS machines in India are owned by just 5 banks — and so is most of the merchant transaction data that comes with them. If you're building merchant credit products (loans to shopkeepers or small businesses) and don't have data partnerships with these top banks, you're working with an incomplete picture. Alternate sources — GST filings, UPI transaction data — can partially fill this gap.
